title: NASA's DART Mission Proves Asteroid Deflection is Possible
summary: In September 2022, NASA's DART (Double Asteroid Redirection Test) mission intentionally crashed a spacecraft into the asteroid moonlet Dimorphos, part of a binary system with the larger asteroid Didymos. The primary goal was to test kinetic impactor technology for planetary defense. Initial results showed the collision shortened Dimorphos' orbit around Didymos by 33 minutes. New research published in Science Advances reveals that the impact also altered the entire binary system's orbit around the Sun by a tiny amount—about 0.15 seconds in its 770-day orbital period. This marks the first human-made change to a natural object's solar orbit. The debris ejected from Dimorphos during the impact provided extra momentum, roughly doubling the force of the collision. Scientists measured a speed change of 11.7 microns per second, which, over time, could accumulate to deflect a hazardous asteroid away from Earth. This demonstrates that kinetic impactors could be an effective method to protect Earth if threatening near-Earth objects are detected early enough.

background_read: Asteroids are remnants from the early solar system, and some, called near-Earth objects (NEOs), have orbits that cross Earth's path, posing impact risks. NASA's DART mission, launched in 2021, targeted the binary asteroid system Didymos and Dimorphos, which orbits about 6.8 million miles from Earth. Kinetic impactors work by transferring momentum from a spacecraft to an asteroid upon collision, potentially altering its trajectory. The DART impact was observed by telescopes and a follow-up mission, Hera, to study the effects in detail. This research contributes to international efforts in planetary defense, coordinated by organizations like NASA's Planetary Defense Coordination Office, to develop strategies for mitigating asteroid threats.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The DART mission successfully altered Dimorphos' orbit around Didymos and the binary system's solar orbit, demonstrating kinetic impactor effectiveness for planetary defense. Purpose: To validate technology for deflecting hazardous asteroids and provide evidence for future defense strategies. Evidence Evaluation: High-quality data from precise measurements published in peer-reviewed journals, supported by debris analysis showing momentum enhancement. Author Credibility: Scientists from NASA and universities with expertise in astronomy and planetary science. Methodology: Tracking orbital changes using telescopes and calculating effects from the impact and ejected material.

perspectives.description: The mission proves that kinetic impactors can reliably change asteroid trajectories, offering a practical defense method against NEO threats.
